 id: FEAT-004\n4\t  depends_on:\n5\t    - helix.prd\n6\t---\n7\t# Feature: Beads (Work Item Tracker)\n8\t\n9\t**ID:** FEAT-004\n10\t**Status:** Complete\n11\t**Priority:** P0\n12\t**Owner:** DDx Team\n13\t\n14\t## Overview\n15\t\n16\tBeads are portable, ephemeral work items with metadata. The `ddx bead` command provides CRUD, dependency tracking, and JSONL interchange — giving AI agents and workflows a structured work queue without coupling to any specific methodology.\n17\t\n18\tThe name follows the `bd` (Dolt-backed) and `br` (SQLite-backed) convention: short, lowercase tool names for the same conceptual unit. DDx beads use JSONL-backed local storage; `bd` and `br` provide database-backed alternatives. All three interchange via JSONL.\n19\t\n20\t## Problem Statement\n21\t\n22\t**Current situation:** AI agents need a work queue to know what to do next. HELIX built its own tracker (`scripts/tracker.sh`) tightly coupled to HELIX workflow semantics (phase labels, spec-id enforcement). Other workflows have no reusable work-tracking primitive.\n23\t\n24\t**Pain points:**\n25\t- No generic work-item tool — every workflow reinvents issue tracking\n26\t- HELIX tracker can't be used without HELIX's full label/validation requirements\n27\t- No portable format for carrying work items between projects and tools\n28\t- Agents can't discover ready work without workflow-specific logic\n29\t\n30\t**Desired outcome:** A lightweight, workflow-agnostic bead tracker that any methodology can layer semantics on top of. HELIX adds phase labels; a kanban workflow adds columns; a plain project just tracks tasks.\n31\t\n32\t## Requirements\n33\t\n34\t### Functional\n35\t\n36\t1. **Bead CRUD** (`ddx bead create/show/update/close`) — create, read, update, and close work items\n37\t2. **Listing and filtering** (`ddx bead list`) — filter by status, label, or custom predicates\n38\t3. **Dependency DAG** (`ddx bead dep add/remove/tree`) — declare ordering constraints between beads\n39\t4. **Ready queue** (`ddx bead ready`) — list `status=open` beads with all dependencies satisfied and no execution-suppressing metadata\n40\t5. **Dependency-waiting query** (`ddx bead blocked`) — list `status=open` beads with unsatisfied dependencies; this is derived waiting, not `status=blocked`\n41\t6. **Status summary** (`ddx bead status`) — counts persisted-status values and derived buckets such as ready and dependency-waiting\n42\t7. **Import** (`ddx bead import`) — ingest beads from `bd`, `br`, or raw JSONL files\n43\t8. **Export** (`ddx bead export`) — write beads as JSONL for interchange with other tools\n44\t9. **Initialization** (`ddx bead init`) — create storage file and directory\n45\t10. **Validation hooks** — workflows register custom validators for create/update operations\n46\t11. **Claim ownership** (`ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --claim [--assignee A]`) — claim a bead with explicit assignee control and stable claim metadata\n47\t12. **Execution evidence** (`ddx bead evidence add/list`) — append-only history for close summaries, agent outputs, and experiment outcomes\n48\t13. **Unknown field preservation** — round-trip fields DDx doesn't know about (enables workflow-specific extensions)\n49\t14. **In-priority queue ordering override** (`ddx bead queue ...`) — let operators move a bead within its existing priority bucket without changing priority or extending the core bead schema\n50\t\n51\t### Non-Functional\n52\t\n53\t- **Performance:** All local operations complete in \u003c100ms for up to 10,000 beads. Read-heavy queue commands (`list`, `ready`, `blocked`, `status`) operate on one parsed snapshot and remain deterministic under repeated calls.\n54\t- **Portability:** No external dependencies beyond the `ddx` binary\n55\t- **Concurrency:** File-level locking prevents corruption from parallel writes. Writers use temp-file + atomic rename semantics, and repair flows keep a backup before swapping in a cleaned file.\n56\t- **Recovery:** Malformed or partially written JSONL records are handled best-effort. Valid records are preserved, malformed lines are reported with line numbers, and a `.bak` backup is kept when the store auto-repairs a file.\n57\t- **Interchange:** JSONL format compatible with `bd`, `br`, and HELIX tracker\n58\t\n59\t## Bead Schema\n60\t\n61\t| Field | Type | Required | Default | Notes |\n62\t|-------|------|----------|---------|-------|\n63\t| id | string | auto-generated | `bx-` + 8 hex chars | Prefix configurable |\n64\t| title | string | yes | — | Non-empty |\n65\t| type | string | no | `task` | Free-form (task, epic, bug, chore, etc.) |\n66\t| status | string | no | `open` | Enum: open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled (bd/br canonical set; see TD-027 §1) |\n67\t| priority | int | no | 2 | Range 0-4 (0 = highest) |\n68\t| labels | []string | no | [] | Free-form, no enforcement |\n69\t| parent | string | no | \"\" | ID of parent bead |\n70\t| description | string | no | \"\" | |\n71\t| acceptance | string | no | \"\" | |\n72\t| deps | []string | no | [] | IDs of dependency beads |\n73\t| assignee | string | no | \"\" | |\n74\t| notes | string | no | \"\" | |\n75\t| created | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |\n76\t| updated | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |\n77\t\n78\tUnknown fields in imported or existing beads are preserved on read/write. This allows HELIX to store `spec-id`, `execution-eligible`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`, `superseded-by`, `replaces`, and DDx-specific queue metadata such as `queue-rank` without extending the core bd/br-compatible schema table.\n79\t\n80\tOperator-attention work uses `status=proposed` when a bead needs a human\n81\tdecision before autonomous execution. Legacy/backcompat `needs_human` labels\n82\tand legacy/backcompat `needs-human-*` preserved `Extra` fields may be read\n83\tduring migration, but they are metadata only; they do not define a queue lane or\n84\tsuppress routing after the status-owned lifecycle contract in TD-027 is applied.\n85\t\n86\tTD-027 §3 (Category Taxonomy) defines how lifecycle actions use the existing carriers: persisted\n87\tbd/br statuses, labels, dependency edges, append-only events, and preserved\n88\t`Extra` fields. FEAT-004 does not introduce additional status vocabulary for\n89\tno_changes, cooldown, superseded, or execution-readiness cases.\n90\t\n91\t### Lifecycle Migration Gate\n92\t\n93\tThe status-owned lifecycle migration is one-way. Legacy/backcompat\n94\t`needs_human`, `triage:needs-investigation`, and pseudo-status lifecycle names\n95\tare input to `ddx bead migrate --lifecycle`; normal runtime does not preserve\n96\tthem as compatibility queue lanes.\n97\t\n98\tBefore queue commands derive readiness or mutate beads, DDx performs the TD-027\n99\tlifecycle startup preflight. If the active store still contains unmigrated\n100\tlegacy lifecycle labels or pseudo-statuses, `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead\n101\tblocked`, `ddx bead status`, `ddx work`, and worker/API queue-readiness surfaces\n102\tmust refuse normal operation. The permitted bead commands during this state are\n103\tthe migration path and diagnostics:\n104\t\n105\t```bash\n106\tddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run\n107\tddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply\n108\t```\n109\t\n110\tThe refusal output must include counts by legacy label/pseudo-status, a sample\n111\tof affected bead IDs, and the exact migration command to run. Because beads are\n112\tgit-tracked, recovery from an incorrect one-way migration is git rollback of\n113\tthe tracker commit rather than maintaining dual lifecycle semantics.\n114\t\n115\tBead readiness assessment uses those existing metadata carriers. Lint/rubric\n116\tscoring happens inside readiness, and post-attempt triage happens after\n117\texecution. None of them add dedicated readiness fields or new schema fields;\n118\treadiness is derived from the bead record's existing title, description,\n119\tacceptance, labels, parent, dependencies, claim metadata, and preserved\n120\textras.\n121\t\n122\t### Queue Semantics For Epics\n123\t\n124\tEpics remain first-class beads, but they are not consumed by the same\n125\tqueue-drain contract as ordinary executable task/bug/chore beads.\n126\t\n127\t- A normal execution-ready queue is **single-ticket-first**. Ready non-epic\n128\t  beads are ordered ahead of ready epic beads at the same priority.\n129\t- Open epics are not launched by the ordinary `ddx work` single-ticket worker.\n130\t  Their disposition is decided by the **idle-path auto-remediator** (see FEAT-010\n131\t  Layer 3 — Idle-Path Diagnosis and Auto-Remediation), which classifies each\n132\t  ready epic by its open/closed child counts, parent/dep state, and operator\n133\t  override labels, then fires exactly one of these auto-actions per loop pass:\n134\t  - **Closure cascade** when all children are closed (`openChildCount == 0 \u0026\u0026\n135\t    totalChildCount \u003e 0`): the epic is treated as a closure candidate and the\n136\t    existing closure-evaluation path auto-closes it.\n137\t  - **Supersession cascade** when an open child carries `superseded-by:\u003cY\u003e`\n138\t    and Y is closed, with no other reason for the child to stay open: the\n139\t    child auto-closes via a one-hop cascade inside `Close()`.\n140\t  - **Auto-decomposition** when the epic has `totalChildCount == 0`, has a\n141\t    valid decomposition source (description with PROBLEM/PROPOSED FIX/AC\n142\t    sections, `spec:*` or `area:*` label), and has not exhausted its\n143\t    per-bead attempt cap or `--max-recovery-cost` budget: the in-loop\n144\t    pre-claim decomposer is dispatched against the epic.\n145\t  - **Operator surface** when none of the above applies (real dependency\n146\t    block on open work, parent/child state conflict, exhausted attempts,\n147\t    operator-set `manual-hold` / `no-auto-decompose` / `container` label,\n148\t    or unknown state): the epic is listed in `ddx work focus` Section A\n149\t    (operator-required). Beads with an auto-action queued for the next loop\n150\t    pass are surfaced in `ddx work focus` Section B (count with `--verbose`\n151\t    detail) rather than silently hidden.\n152\t- Child beads of an epic remain individually executable units and may be\n153\t  closed one-by-one.\n154\t- Epic queue entries remain visible in the tracker and UI. The diagnostic\n155\t  surface `ddx work plan --explain` prints the per-epic reason code so\n156\t  operators can see what the loop will do on its next iteration.\n157\t\n158\tThe \"skip and exit\" disposition (`No execution-eligible beads in the queue`\n159\twith only ready epics present) is forbidden by the FEAT-010 reliability\n160\tcontract; every ready epic must classify into one of the four auto-actions\n161\tabove. Concurrency, cycle guards, idempotency, and audit-event requirements\n162\tare owned by FEAT-010 Layer 3 and are common to every auto-remediation in the\n163\tidle path.\n164\t\n165\t**Deferred** (out of scope for this contract): an epic-scoped worker mode\n166\twith a dedicated epic branch/worktree that sequentially executes child beads\n167\ton a shared branch. That capability is a separate, larger initiative and is\n168\tnot required for the queue-drain contract to hold.\n169\t\n170\t### Queue Ordering Overrides\n171\t\n172\tThe canonical ready-queue order is:\n173\t\n174\t1. `priority` ascending (`0` first, `4` last)\n175\t2. explicit `queue-rank` ascending, with missing `queue-rank` sorted after\n176\t   explicit ranks inside the same priority bucket\n177\t3. `created_at` ascending\n178\t4. `id` ascending\n179\t\n180\t`queue-rank` is preserved extension metadata stored in the bead's unknown-field\n181\tmap, not a core bead schema field. It is an operator override for ordering\n182\twithin one priority bucket only. A ranked `P1` bead never sorts ahead of an\n183\tunranked `P0` bead, and a ranked bead that is blocked, on retry cooldown,\n184\tsuperseded, `execution-eligible=false`, or epic-only/container work remains\n185\texcluded from `ddx work`'s execution-ready picker.\n186\t\n187\tThe CLI exposes queue movement as a first-class surface rather than requiring\n188\toperators to manage raw metadata:\n189\t\n190\t- `ddx bead queue top \u003cid\u003e` assigns a rank that places the bead first among\n191\t  ready beads with the same priority.\n192\t- `ddx bead queue move \u003cid\u003e --before \u003cother-id\u003e` places the bead before another\n193\t  bead in the same priority bucket.\n194\t- `ddx bead queue move \u003cid\u003e --after \u003cother-id\u003e` places the bead after another\n195\t  bead in the same priority bucket.\n196\t- `ddx bead queue clear \u003cid\u003e` removes the explicit rank and restores the\n197\t  default tie-break ordering for that bead.\n198\t\n199\t`queue move --before/--after` fails when the two beads have different\n200\tpriorities. Operators that want to change urgency must use `ddx bead update\n201\t\u003cid\u003e --priority N` explicitly. Queue-rank values are canonicalized as integers;\n202\tread paths may accept numeric strings for compatibility, but writes persist a\n203\tnumber. Rank calculation should use sparse integer values and renormalize only\n204\tthe affected priority bucket when no midpoint exists.\n205\t\n206\t## Storage\n207\t\n208\tDDx beads abstracts over multiple storage backends. Consumers (HELIX, dun, other workflows) **must only interact via `ddx bead` commands or the server API** — never read/write the storage file directly.\n209\t\n210\t### Backend Selection\n211\t\n212\t| Backend | Config Value | When To Use |\n213\t|---------|-------------|-------------|\n214\t| `jsonl` (default) | `bead.backend: jsonl` | Simple projects, single-agent, no external tooling |\n215\t| `bd` | `bead.backend: bd` | Dolt-backed bead store (collaborative, branching) |\n216\t| `br` | `bead.backend: br` | SQLite-backed bead store (local performance) |\n217\t\n218\tBackend is configured in `.ddx/config.yaml` or via `DDX_BEAD_BACKEND` env var. Default: `jsonl`.\n219\t\n220\tFor `bd` and `br` backends, DDx shells out to the respective binary. For `jsonl`, DDx manages the file directly.\n221\t\n222\t### JSONL Backend Details\n223\t\n224\t- **Default path:** `.ddx/beads.jsonl` (one JSON object per line, sorted by id)\n225\t- **Configuration:** `DDX_BEAD_DIR` env var or `bead.dir` in `.ddx/config.yaml` (default: `.ddx`)\n226\t- **Locking:** Directory-based lock at `.ddx/beads.lock/` with PID file and acquisition timestamp. Configurable timeout (default: 10s).\n227\t- **Write algorithm:** Mutating operations take the lock, build a complete bead snapshot, write to `beads.jsonl.tmp`, and atomically rename the temp file into place. This avoids partially written tracker state.\n228\t- **Read algorithm:** Queue commands scan `beads.jsonl` line-by-line. Valid JSON objects are loaded into a snapshot; malformed lines are skipped with line-numbered warnings so one bad record does not take down the entire queue.\n229\t- **Repair algorithm:** If a read finds malformed lines but at least one valid bead, DDx takes the store lock, copies the current file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak`, and rewrites the repaired snapshot atomically.\n230\t- **Failure mode:** If every line is malformed, the command returns a contextual error that names the file and malformed-record count.\n231\t- **Git integration:** `beads.jsonl` is project state and must be committed to git. Auto-commit behavior after bead mutations is defined in FEAT-012 (Git Awareness), requirements S7 and US-124. `ddx init` generates agent guidance that includes `beads.jsonl` as a committable file (FEAT-012 S8, US-125).\n232\t- **Design reference:** See [`SD-004-beads-tracker.md`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-004-beads-tracker.md), [`TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md), and [`TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../03-test/test-plans/TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md) for the concrete algorithms, repair flow, claim semantics, evidence trail, and validation matrix.\n233\t\n234\t### Storage Boundary\n235\t\n236\tThe bead backend is the reusable DDx storage engine for bead-schema records.\n237\tThe primary work queue is one collection, not the only possible collection.\n238\t\n239\t- The default active-work collection maps to `beads.jsonl` in the JSONL backend.\n240\t- Other DDx services may use separate bead-backed collections for archived\n241\t  work, execution history, agent session indexes, or other record families.\n242\t- Large payloads such as prompt bodies or raw logs live in separate attachment\n243\t  files referenced from the bead-schema record rather than inline in the\n244\t  primary collection row.\n245\t- Backend selection is a store-level concern, not a single-file concern.\n246\t\n247\t### ID Prefix\n248\t\n249\tFollowing the bd convention, the prefix is **derived from the repository name** by default:\n250\t\n251\t- **Auto-detected:** git repo root directory name (e.g., repo `my-project` → prefix `my-project` → IDs like `my-project-a3f2dd`)\n252\t- **Override:** `bead.id_prefix` in `.ddx/config.yaml` or `DDX_BEAD_PREFIX` env var\n253\t- **Fallback:** `bx` if repo name detection fails\n254\t- **Format:** `\u003cprefix\u003e-` + 8 hex chars\n255\t\n256\tThis matches how bd derives its prefix, ensuring beads created by DDx and bd in the same repo use the same naming convention.\n257\t\n258\t## Validation\n259\t\n260\t**DDx base validation** (always applied):\n261\t- Title is non-empty\n262\t- Dependencies reference existing bead IDs\n263\t- No self-referential dependencies\n264\t- Priority is in valid range (0-4)\n265\t- Status is a recognized value\n266\t\n267\t**Workflow validation hooks:** An executable at `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-create` (and `validate-bead-update`) receives the bead JSON on stdin. Exit codes: 0 = ok, 1 = hard error (stderr = message, creation blocked), 2 = warning (stderr = message, creation proceeds).\n268\t\n269\t### Bead Readiness And Authoring Quality\n270\t\n271\tADR-023 adds lifecycle-quality hooks on top of the existing validation-hook\n272\tsurface. The canonical product concept is bead readiness assessment: the\n273\tpre-claim decision about whether a bead is tractable and actionable. The same\n274\tpolicy surface also includes lint/rubric scoring inside readiness, which\n275\tmeasures prompt quality, and post-attempt triage, which classifies evidence\n276\tafter execution. The older \"pre-claim intake\" wording survives only as legacy\n277\tcompatibility language, not normative terminology. Base validation still\n278\tprotects the reusable bead schema; authoring quality checks protect the\n279\t\"bead as prompt\" contract needed by `ddx try`, `ddx work`, and autonomous\n280\tsub-agent execution. Readiness uses the existing bead metadata carriers and\n281\tdoes not add schema fields.\n282\t\n283\tThe lint rubric is the 8-criterion template in\n284\t`docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`:\n285\t\n286\t- title is scoped and imperative\n287\t- description contains problem, root cause with file:line, proposed fix, and\n288\t  non-scope\n289\t- acceptance criteria are numbered, verifiable, and name concrete tests or\n290\t  observable artifacts when tests apply\n291\t- new code paths include wired-in assertions\n292\t- acceptance criteria name the applicable `go test` command and\n293\t  `lefthook run pre-commit`\n294\t- labels include phase, area, kind, and cross-references\n295\t- parent and dependencies are explicit\n296\t- the bead body is sufficient for a competent sub-agent to execute without\n297\t  asking for operator context\n298\t\n299\tBead readiness assessment, lint/rubric scoring, and post-attempt triage all\n300\tinvoke the nested bead-lifecycle skill under the `ddx` skill tree. DDx owns\n301\tpassing bead JSON, mode, waiver labels, and evidence paths into the hook; the\n302\tskill owns producing human-readable criterion findings. Hook output is\n303\tephemeral execution evidence, not durable bead schema. For `ddx try` and\n304\t`ddx work`, the readiness and lint reports are stored under the attempt\n305\tevidence directory alongside the prompt, result, checks, and triage records.\n306\t`beads.jsonl` is not extended with lint-score fields.\n307\t\n308\tWaiver storage uses existing labels. The durable form is\n309\t`lint-waiver:\u003ccriterion\u003e`, such as `lint-waiver:c` for the concrete-test-name\n310\tcriterion. Built-in rubric skips come from the authoring template: doc-only\n311\tbeads may skip test-name and wired-in criteria, epic beads may satisfy those\n312\tcriteria through children, and deletion/rename beads may skip wired-in checks\n313\twhen behavior preservation is asserted.\n314\t\n315\tManual override is explicit and audited. When an operator dispatches with\n316\t`--force --reason \u003ctext\u003e`, DDx appends an evidence event recording the actor,\n317\treason, mode, waived criteria, and lint summary. The override does not mutate\n318\tthe bead schema and does not suppress future lint runs.\n319\t\n320\tWARN-ONLY is the default mode: lint findings are reported but create/update and\n321\tdispatch proceed. BLOCK mode is opt-in and may block dispatch only after valid\n322\tlint output, rubric skips, and label waivers have been applied. Hook\n323\tinfrastructure failures follow ADR-023's fail-open rule and are reported as\n324\twarnings, not schema validation failures.\n325\t\n326\t## CLI Surface\n327\t\n328\t```\n329\tddx bead init\n330\tddx bead create \"Title\" [--type T] [--priority N] [--labels L,L] [--acceptance A] [--parent ID] [--description D]\n331\tddx bead show \u003cid\u003e [--json]\n332\tddx bead update \u003cid\u003e [--title T] [--status S] [--priority N] [--labels L,L] [--acceptance A] [--assignee A] [--claim]\n333\tddx bead queue top \u003cid\u003e\n334\tddx bead queue move \u003cid\u003e [--before OTHER | --after OTHER]\n335\tddx bead queue clear \u003cid\u003e\n336\tddx bead evidence add \u003cid\u003e [--kind K] [--body B] [--summary S] [--source SRC] [--actor A]\n337\tddx bead evidence list \u003cid\u003e [--json]\n338\tddx bead close \u003cid\u003e\n339\tddx bead list [--status S] [--label L] [--json]\n340\tddx bead ready [--json]\n341\tddx bead blocked [--json]\n342\tddx bead status\n343\tddx bead dep add \u003cid\u003e \u003cdep-id\u003e\n344\tddx bead dep remove \u003cid\u003e \u003cdep-id\u003e\n345\tddx bead dep tree [\u003cid\u003e]\n346\tddx bead import [--from auto|bd|br|jsonl] [file]\n347\tddx bead export [--stdout] [file]\n348\t```\n349\t\n350\t## Server Endpoints (FEAT-002 integration)\n351\t\n352\t| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description |\n353\t|----------|--------------|-------------|\n354\t| `ddx_list_beads` | `GET /api/beads` | List beads with optional filters |\n355\t| `ddx_show_bead` | `GET /api/beads/:id` | Show one bead |\n356\t| `ddx_bead_ready` | `GET /api/beads/ready` | List ready beads |\n357\t| `ddx_bead_status` | `GET /api/beads/status` | Summary counts |\n358\t\n359\t## User Stories\n360\t\n361\t### US-020: Create and Manage Beads\n362\t**As a** developer tracking work items\n363\t**I want** to create, update, and close beads from the CLI\n364\t**So that** I have a lightweight work queue without external tools\n365\t\n366\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n367\t- Given I'm in a DDx project, when I run `ddx bead create \"Fix auth bug\" --type bug --priority 1`, then a bead is created with a unique ID and status `open`\n368\t- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --status in_progress --assignee me`, then the bead is updated\n369\t- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead close \u003cid\u003e`, then its status is set to `closed`\n370\t- Given beads exist, when I run `ddx bead list --status open`, then only open beads are shown\n371\t\n372\t### US-021: Track Dependencies Between Beads\n373\t**As a** developer with ordered work\n374\t**I want** to declare that bead A must complete before bead B\n375\t**So that** the ready queue reflects actual execution order\n376\t\n377\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n378\t- Given beads A and B exist, when I run `ddx bead dep add B A`, then B depends on A\n379\t- Given B depends on open A, when I run `ddx bead ready`, then B is not listed\n380\t- Given B depends on A and A is closed, when I run `ddx bead ready`, then B is listed\n381\t- Given I run `ddx bead dep tree`, then I see the full dependency graph\n382\t\n383\t### US-022: Import Beads from External Sources\n384\t**As a** developer migrating from another tracker\n385\t**I want** to import beads from `bd`, `br`, or JSONL files\n386\t**So that** I can consolidate work items into DDx\n387\t\n388\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n389\t- Given a `.beads/issues.jsonl` file exists, when I run `ddx bead import --from jsonl`, then beads are imported preserving all fields\n390\t- Given `bd` is available and has data, when I run `ddx bead import --from bd`, then beads are imported\n391\t- Given I run `ddx bead export --stdout`, then JSONL output is compatible with `ddx bead import --from jsonl`\n392\t\n393\t### US-023: Agent Queries Ready Beads via MCP\n394\t**As an** AI agent with MCP tool access\n395\t**I want** to query the ready queue programmatically\n396\t**So that** I can pick up the next actionable work item\n397\t\n398\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n399\t- Given ddx-server is running with beads, when an agent calls `ddx_bead_ready`, then it receives ready beads as structured JSON\n400\t- Given an agent calls `ddx_show_bead` with an ID, then it receives the full bead including all fields (known and unknown)\n401\t\n402\t### US-023a: Operator Reorders Work Within Priority\n403\t**As an** operator managing the ready queue\n404\t**I want** to move a bead ahead of other beads with the same priority\n405\t**So that** I can express immediate sequence without changing urgency or rewriting the core bead schema\n406\t\n407\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n408\t- Given two ready `P0` beads exist, when I run `ddx bead queue top \u003clater-id\u003e`, then `ddx bead ready --execution` lists `\u003clater-id\u003e` before the other `P0` bead\n409\t- Given a `P1` bead has `queue-rank=0` and a `P0` bead has no `queue-rank`, when I run `ddx bead ready --execution`, then the `P0` bead remains before the `P1` bead\n410\t- Given two beads with different priorities, when I run `ddx bead queue move \u003cid\u003e --before \u003cother-id\u003e`, then DDx returns an error explaining that queue moves are limited to one priority bucket\n411\t- Given a ranked bead is blocked, superseded, on retry cooldown, `execution-eligible=false`, or an epic-only/container bead, then `ddx bead ready --execution` still excludes it\n412\t- Given a ranked bead exists, when I run `ddx bead queue clear \u003cid\u003e`, then DDx removes `queue-rank` and the bead returns to the default priority/created/id ordering\n413\t\n414\t### US-024: Operator Recovers From Partial JSONL Corruption\n415\t**As a** repo operator\n416\t**I want** bead queue commands to survive a partially written or externally edited `beads.jsonl`\n417\t**So that** one bad record does not block the whole queue\n418\t\n419\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n420\t- Given `beads.jsonl` contains one malformed record and one valid record, when I run `ddx bead ready` or `ddx bead status`, then DDx reports the malformed line number, preserves the valid record, and rewrites the cleaned file atomically\n421\t- Given DDx auto-repairs a partially corrupted file, then it keeps a `.bak` backup of the original contents\n422\t- Given `beads.jsonl` contains only malformed records, then queue reads fail with a contextual error that includes the file path and malformed-record count\n423\t\n424\t### US-025: Operator Claims a Bead With a Specific Assignee\n425\t**As a** developer or agent coordinating work\n426\t**I want** to claim a bead and assign it to a known actor\n427\t**So that** ownership is explicit and not hardcoded to DDx\n428\t\n429\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n430\t- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --claim --assignee alice`, then the bead becomes `in_progress`, `assignee` is `alice`, and `claimed-at` / `claimed-pid` are recorded\n431\t- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --claim` without `--assignee`, then DDx uses the runtime caller identity fallback before defaulting to `ddx`\n432\t- Given a claimed bead exists, when I run `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --unclaim`, then claim metadata is cleared and the bead returns to `open`\n433\t\n434\t### US-026: Operator Appends Execution Evidence\n435\t**As a** developer or agent\n436\t**I want** to append evidence to a bead without rewriting prior history\n437\t**So that** close summaries and experiment outcomes remain auditable\n438\t\n439\t**Acceptance Criteria:**\n440\t- Given a bead exists, when I run `ddx bead evidence add \u003cid\u003e --kind summary --body \"done\"`, then a new immutable event is appended with a timestamp and actor\n441\t- Given evidence already exists, when I append another event, then prior entries remain in order and are not rewritten\n442\t- Given I run `ddx bead show \u003cid\u003e --json`, then the full evidence history is returned\n443\t- Given I run `ddx bead list`, `ready`, `blocked`, or `status`, then evidence entries do not change queue semantics\n444\t\n445\t## Claim Semantics\n446\t\n447\tBeads support advisory ownership claims for agent/workflow coordination:\n448\t\n449\t- `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --claim [--assignee NAME]` sets `status=in_progress`, resolves `assignee` from the explicit flag or runtime caller identity, and records `claimed-at` (ISO-8601 UTC) plus `claimed-pid` (current PID)\n450\t- `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --unclaim` sets `status=open`, clears `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`\n451\t- Claims are advisory — they prevent double-claiming, not hard locks\n452\t- `claimed-at` and `claimed-pid` are standard fields (not unknown-field extensions)\n453\t\n454\tClaim resolution order is:\n455\t\n456\t1. Explicit `--assignee` flag.\n457\t2. Runtime caller identity from the execution context.\n458\t3. `ddx` as the final fallback.\n459\t\n460\tThis makes claim ownership explicit for both human operators and agents without hardcoding the caller to the CLI binary name.\n461\t\n462\t## Execution Evidence\n463\t\n464\tBeads carry append-only execution evidence in workflow-specific metadata at `Extra[\"events\"]`.\n465\t\n466\t- Each event records `kind`, `summary`, `body`, `actor`, `created_at`, and `source`.\n467\t- `kind` covers common workflow events such as `claim`, `unclaim`, `close`, `summary`, and `experiment`.\n468\t- Events are appended in order and never rewritten or removed by normal CLI operations.\n469\t- Evidence is visible in `show --json` and server/API responses via the `events` metadata field, but queue derivation ignores it.\n470\t- Workflows may use the evidence trail for operator notes, experiment results, or close summaries without changing the bead's core status semantics.\n471\t\n472\t## Custom Fields\n473\t\n474\tWorkflows need to store fields DDx doesn't know about (e.g., HELIX stores `spec-id`, `execution-eligible`, `superseded-by`).\n475\t\n476\t- `ddx bead create --set key=value` — set arbitrary field on create\n477\t- `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --set key=value` — set arbitrary field on update\n478\t- `ddx bead update \u003cid\u003e --set closing_commit_sha=\u003csha\u003e` — repair closing provenance on a closed bead without editing storage directly\n479\t- `ddx bead list --where key=value` — filter by custom field\n480\t- Unknown fields are preserved on all read/write operations\n481\t- Custom fields appear in JSON output and import/export\n482\t- The evidence trail is stored as workflow-specific metadata in `Extra[\"events\"]`, preserving bd/br compatibility while still round-tripping the full history.\n483\t\n484\tThis keeps DDx agnostic while giving workflows a typed pass-through mechanism.\n485\t\n486\t## Validation Hooks\n487\t\n488\tWorkflows register custom validators as executables:\n489\t\n490\t- `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-create` — called on create, receives bead JSON on stdin\n491\t- `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-update` — called on update, receives bead JSON on stdin\n492\t- Exit 0 = ok, Exit 1 = hard error (creation blocked, stderr = message), Exit 2 = warning (proceeds, stderr = message)\n493\t\n494\tExample: HELIX installs a hook requiring `spec-id` on task-type beads and `acceptance` on task/epic beads.\n495\t\n496\t## Edge Cases\n497\t\n498\t- Create when storage doesn't exist — auto-initialize on first write\n499\t- Import with duplicate IDs — skip duplicates, report count\n500\t- Dep add with nonexistent target — error with clear message\n501\t- Concurrent writes — locking prevents corruption, second writer waits or times out\n502\t- Partially corrupted JSONL — valid records are preserved, malformed lines are skipped with contextual warnings, and auto-repair keeps a `.bak` backup before atomic replacement\n503\t- Export when no beads exist — produce empty JSONL file\n504\t- Unknown fields in imported data — preserve on round-trip without validation\n505\t\n506\t## Dependencies\n507\t\n508\t- `.ddx/` directory (created by `ddx init` or `ddx bead init`)\n509\t- `bd` / `br` only required for `--from bd` / `--from br` import\n510\t\n511\t## Out of Scope\n512\t\n513\t- Workflow-specific validation (phase labels, spec-id) — that's HELIX or other workflows via hooks\n514\t- Supervisory execution loops — that's workflow-level orchestration\n515\t- Real-time sync between bead stores — use import/export Any new pattern added here is automatically picked up by\n21\t// IsInfrastructureFailure callers.\n22\tvar InfrastructureFailurePatterns = []string{\n23\t\t// HTTP-level provider unavailability\n24\t\t\"502\", \"503\", \"504\",\n25\t\t\"bad gateway\",\n26\t\t\"service unavailable\",\n27\t\t\"gateway timeout\",\n28\t\t// Network-level unreachability\n29\t\t\"connection refused\",\n30\t\t\"no such host\",\n31\t\t\"no route to host\",\n32\t\t\"network is unreachable\",\n33\t\t\"i/o timeout\",\n34\t\t\"context deadline exceeded\",\n35\t\t// Auth/quota exhaustion (operator-fixable, not a model fault)\n36\t\t\"401\", \"429\",\n37\t\t\"unauthorized\",\n38\t\t\"rate limit\",\n39\t\t\"ratelimit\",\n40\t\t\"quota exceeded\",\n41\t\t\"insufficient quota\",\n42\t\t\"insufficient_quota\",\n43\t\t// Subprocess harness binary missing or unstartable\n44\t\t\"command not found\",\n45\t\t\"executable file not found\",\n46\t\t\"no such file or directory\",\n47\t\t// Upstream routing/service selection failures\n48\t\t\"resolveroute:\",\n49\t\t\"no viable routing candidate\",\n50\t\t\"no live provider supports\",\n51\t\t\"no candidate satisfying local endpoint\",\n52\t}\n53\t\n54\t// IsInfrastructureFailure reports whether the given failure status + detail\n55\t// indicates a transient infrastructure problem the model could not have\n56\t// fixed. Only execution_failed can be infrastructure; other escalatable\n57\t// statuses are semantic outcomes that should proceed through the powerClass ladder.\n58\t// Returns false for statuses whose detail does not match any known\n59\t// infrastructure pattern.\n60\t//\n61\t// Used by the work to decide whether to (a) defer the bead with a\n62\t// retry-after and try the same powerClass later, or (b) burn through to the next\n63\t// powerClass as the standard escalation policy.\n64\tfunc IsInfrastructureFailure(status, detail string) bool {\n65\t\tif status != \"execution_failed\" {\n66\t\t\treturn false\n67\t\t}\n68\t\tif detail == \"\" {\n69\t\t\treturn false\n70\t\t}\n71\t\tlower := strings.ToLower(detail)\n72\t\tfor _, p := range InfrastructureFailurePatterns {\n73\t\t\tif strings.Contains(lower, p) {\n74\t\t\t\treturn true\n75\t\t\t}\n76\t\t}\n77\t\treturn false\n78\t}\n79\t\n80\t// DefaultMaxCostUSD is the per-work dollar cap on accumulated billed\n81\t// spend. Exceeding the cap halts further bead claiming so an work\n82\t// run cannot silently burn through arbitrary openrouter / pay-per-token\n83\t// credits. Local LLMs and subscription-bundled providers do not count\n84\t// toward the cap (see CountsTowardCostCap).\n85\t//\n86\t// $100 is large enough not to interrupt normal subscription-driven work\n87\t// (where billed cost stays at $0) and small enough to surface a clear stop\n88\t// signal before runaway openrouter usage.\n89\tconst DefaultMaxCostUSD = 100.0\n90\t\n91\t// DefaultMaxBeadCostUSD is the default per-bead budget. When a single bead\n92\t// exhausts this amount across all escalation powerClasses, escalation stops and the\n93\t// bead is returned to open without a cooldown. Operators can override\n94\t// per-bead with a budget:\u003cUSD\u003e label (e.g. budget:25.0).\n95\t//\n96\t// $5 is a conservative default — enough to attempt two or three smart-powerClass\n97\t// runs before halting, but low enough to protect against runaway escalation\n98\t// on a pathologically difficult bead.\n99\tconst DefaultMaxBeadCostUSD = 5.0\n100\t\n101\t// DefaultMaxRecoveryCostUSD is the per-bead recovery budget for automated\n102\t// reframe/decompose attempts after repeated ladder exhaustion.\n103\tconst DefaultMaxRecoveryCostUSD = 2.0\n104\t\n105\t// PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason is the detail-string prefix written into a\n106\t// report when the per-bead budget is exceeded. The work detects this\n107\t// marker to handle the outcome correctly (unclaim, event, counter increment,\n108\t// no cooldown).\n109\tconst PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason = \"per-bead budget exhausted\"\n110\t\n111\t// CostClassBilled is the HarnessInfo.CostClass value the agent uses for\n112\t// pay-per-token providers. Free local providers report \"free\" and\n113\t// subscription-bundled providers report \"subscription\".\n114\tconst CostClassBilled = \"expensive\"\n115\t\n116\t// CountsTowardCostCap returns true when an attempt's reported CostUSD\n117\t// should accumulate against the loop's MaxCostUSD. Callers pass the\n118\t// HarnessInfo of the harness that ran the attempt; when info is nil the\n119\t// safe default is to count (treat unknown as billed).\n120\tfunc CountsTowardCostCap(isLocal, isSubscription bool, costClass string) bool {\n121\t\tif isLocal {\n122\t\t\treturn false\n123\t\t}\n124\t\tif isSubscription {\n125\t\t\treturn false\n126\t\t}\n127\t\t// CostClass \"free\" or \"subscription\" never bill; only \"expensive\" does.\n128\t\tif costClass != \"\" \u0026\u0026 costClass != CostClassBilled {\n129\t\t\treturn false\n130\t\t}\n131\t\treturn true\n132\t}\n133\t\n134\t// HarnessBilledLookup reports whether a harness's reported CostUSD should\n135\t// be accumulated against a CostCapTracker. Implementations typically\n136\t// consult a service.ListHarnesses snapshot. A nil lookup is treated as\n137\t// \"count by default\" (the safe option for unknown harnesses).\n138\ttype HarnessBilledLookup func(harnessName string) bool\n139\t\n140\t// CostCapTracker accumulates billed cost for a single work run\n141\t// and reports when accumulated spend has reached the configured cap.\n142\t// Safe for concurrent use across goroutines (workers may call Add and\n143\t// Tripped concurrently).\n144\t//\n145\t// MaxUSD == 0 disables the cap; Tripped always returns (_, false). The\n146\t// Lookup callback decides which harnesses contribute to the running\n147\t// total — local and subscription-bundled providers should be excluded\n148\t// (see CountsTowardCostCap).\n149\ttype CostCapTracker struct {\n150\t\tMaxUSD float64\n151\t\tLookup HarnessBilledLookup\n152\t\n153\t\tmu    sync.Mutex\n154\t\tspent float64\n155\t\t// cache memoizes Lookup results so we do not rebuild a service for\n156\t\t// every reported attempt. Protected by mu.\n157\t\tcache map[string]bool\n158\t}\n159\t\n160\t// NewCostCapTracker constructs a tracker with the given dollar cap and\n161\t// lookup. Pass maxUSD \u003c= 0 to disable the cap. A nil lookup is treated\n162\t// as \"count by default\" — every harness contributes.\n163\tfunc NewCostCapTracker(maxUSD float64, lookup HarnessBilledLookup) *CostCapTracker {\n164\t\treturn \u0026CostCapTracker{\n165\t\t\tMaxUSD: maxUSD,\n166\t\t\tLookup: lookup,\n167\t\t\tcache:  map[string]bool{},\n168\t\t}\n169\t}\n170\t\n171\t// counts reports whether harnessName's CostUSD should accumulate. The\n172\t// result is memoized; callers that want fresh resolution should\n173\t// construct a new tracker. Lookups for empty harness names default to\n174\t// true (count) so accidentally-missing harness metadata never silently\n175\t// bypasses the cap.\n176\tfunc (t *CostCapTracker) counts(harnessName string) bool {\n177\t\tif harnessName == \"\" {\n178\t\t\treturn true\n179\t\t}\n180\t\tt.mu.Lock()\n181\t\tif v, ok := t.cache[harnessName]; ok {\n182\t\t\tt.mu.Unlock()\n183\t\t\treturn v\n184\t\t}\n185\t\tt.mu.Unlock()\n186\t\tresult := true\n187\t\tif t.Lookup != nil {\n188\t\t\tresult = t.Lookup(harnessName)\n189\t\t}\n190\t\tt.mu.Lock()\n191\t\tt.cache[harnessName] = result\n192\t\tt.mu.Unlock()\n193\t\treturn result\n194\t}\n195\t\n196\t// Add accumulates costUSD against the running total when harnessName's\n197\t// billing class counts toward the cap. Non-positive cost is ignored.\n198\tfunc (t *CostCapTracker) Add(harnessName string, costUSD float64) {\n199\t\tif costUSD \u003c= 0 {\n200\t\t\treturn\n201\t\t}\n202\t\tif !t.counts(harnessName) {\n203\t\t\treturn\n204\t\t}\n205\t\tt.mu.Lock()\n206\t\tt.spent += costUSD\n207\t\tt.mu.Unlock()\n208\t}\n209\t\n210\t// Spent returns the current accumulated billed total.\n211\tfunc (t *CostCapTracker) Spent() float64 {\n212\t\tt.mu.Lock()\n213\t\tdefer t.mu.Unlock()\n214\t\treturn t.spent\n215\t}\n216\t\n217\t// Tripped reports whether the accumulated spend has reached MaxUSD. When\n218\t// MaxUSD \u003c= 0 the cap is disabled and Tripped returns (_, false).\n219\t// Returns the formatted operator-facing detail string when tripped so\n220\t// callers can populate a stop-the-loop ExecuteBeadReport.\n221\tfunc (t *CostCapTracker) Tripped() (string, bool) {\n222\t\tif t.MaxUSD \u003c= 0 {\n223\t\t\treturn \"\", false\n224\t\t}\n225\t\tt.mu.Lock()\n226\t\tspent := t.spent\n227\t\tt.mu.Unlock()\n228\t\tif spent \u003c t.MaxUSD {\n229\t\t\treturn \"\", false\n230\t\t}\n231\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"cost cap reached: $%.2f billed \u003e= $%.2f cap; raise the cap or set 0 to disable. Subscription and local providers do not count.\", spent, t.MaxUSD), true\n232\t}\n233\t\n234\t// PerBeadCostTracker accumulates billed cost for a single bead across all\n235\t// escalation powerClasses and reports when the per-bead budget is exceeded.\n236\t// When the budget is exceeded, the caller should stop escalating, unclaim\n237\t// the bead, and emit a per_bead_budget_exhausted event (see TD-031 §5).\n238\t//\n239\t// MaxUSD == 0 disables the cap (unlimited). A nil Lookup counts all harnesses.\n240\t// Use ParseBeadBudgetLabel to read a bead-level override from its label set.\n241\ttype PerBeadCostTracker struct {\n242\t\tinner *CostCapTracker\n243\t}\n244\t\n245\t// NewPerBeadCostTracker constructs a per-bead tracker. Pass maxUSD \u003c= 0 for\n246\t// unlimited. Lookup follows the same billing-class convention as CostCapTracker.\n247\tfunc NewPerBeadCostTracker(maxUSD float64, lookup HarnessBilledLookup) *PerBeadCostTracker {\n248\t\treturn \u0026PerBeadCostTracker{inner: NewCostCapTracker(maxUSD, lookup)}\n249\t}\n250\t\n251\t// Add accumulates costUSD when the harness counts toward the cap.\n252\tfunc (t *PerBeadCostTracker) Add(harnessName string, costUSD float64) {\n253\t\tt.inner.Add(harnessName, costUSD)\n254\t}\n255\t\n256\t// Spent returns the current accumulated billed total for this bead.\n257\tfunc (t *PerBeadCostTracker) Spent() float64 {\n258\t\treturn t.inner.Spent()\n259\t}\n260\t\n261\t// Tripped reports whether the per-bead budget is exceeded. Returns a detail\n262\t// string containing PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason when tripped so the execute-\n263\t// loop can detect and handle the outcome.\n264\tfunc (t *PerBeadCostTracker) Tripped() (string, bool) {\n265\t\tif t.inner.MaxUSD \u003c= 0 {\n266\t\t\treturn \"\", false\n267\t\t}\n268\t\tspent := t.inner.Spent()\n269\t\tif spent \u003c t.inner.MaxUSD {\n270\t\t\treturn \"\", false\n271\t\t}\n272\t\treturn fmt.Sprintf(\"%s: $%.2f billed \u003e= $%.2f per-bead budget; set --max-bead-cost=0 to disable or add a budget:\u003cUSD\u003e label to override\", PerBeadBudgetExhaustedReason, spent, t.inner.MaxUSD), true\n273\t}\n274\t\n275\t// ParseBeadBudgetLabel scans a bead's label slice for a \"budget:\u003cfloat\u003e\" entry\n276\t// and returns the parsed value and true when found. An entry with an invalid\n277\t// numeric suffix is ignored (returns 0, false) so the caller falls back to the\n278\t// default per-bead budget rather than disabling the budget entirely.\n279\tfunc ParseBeadBudgetLabel(labels []string) (float64, bool) {\n280\t\tconst prefix = \"budget:\"\n281\t\tfor _, l := range labels {\n282\t\t\tif !strings.HasPrefix(l, prefix) {\n283\t\t\t\tcontinue\n284\t\t\t}\n285\t\t\traw := strings.TrimPrefix(l, prefix)\n286\t\t\tv, err := strconv.ParseFloat(raw, 64)\n287\t\t\tif err != nil || v \u003c 0 {\n288\t\t\t\treturn 0, false\n289\t\t\t}\n290\t\t\treturn v, true\n291\t\t}\n292\t\treturn 0, false\n293\t}\n294\t"}}
